What kind of storage does the cloud data store belong to? RDBMS stands for Relational Database Management System, NoSQL stands for Not only SQL, Blob Store is for storing binary data and CDN stands for Content Delivery Network.

Which cloud deployment models does the cloud data store provide?

Is it possible to create a schema for the data?
What is the guaranteed availability by the provider for the cloud data store itself?
Are there any known limits how big the cloud data store can scale up to (e.g. memory, storage, nodes)?
Is the master node automatically switched in case of a problem with the current master node?
If multi-tenancy features are provided, which types of multi-tenancy are supported?
Which Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) are provided to monitor the cloud data store instances?
What replication type is used to be highly available?
What is the average internal latency for simple read request?
Does the the cloud data store scale by adding better hardwarde (scale up) or by running on more instances (scale out) or a mix of both?
Is the data stored in the cloud data store protected from manipulation?
Are backups provided? If yes, how often can they be triggered?
Different Data Center in Same Region
Partial Automation (Mix of Aut. and Manual)
Different Data Center in Different Region
Usage of highly-scalable data stores (NoSQL, Blob Store)
Work on Copy of Data (Data Analysis and Monitoring)

Similar to backups, creating a copy of parts or of the whole data base layer. Reasons for archiving can be regulatory rules or relief of the production system in case the archived data is also removed from production system.

Plain outsourcing of the DBL means migrating the local DBL to the Cloud without changing the type of the datastore (RDBMS, NoSQL or Blob Store), usually the whole application is migrated as well and data migration is part of the overall Cloud migration.
Remote clients with temporary access to the data base layer can download a copy from the master, go offline, do changes and when network is available synchronize the changes with the master.
Migrate from a RDBMS which is not highly-scalable to a highly scalabe Cloud data store like a NoSQL or Blob store).

Data is split into disjunct parts and moved to different data centers. This is helpful to reduce latency by moving the data closer to the client and additionally increase (horizontal) scalability. Horizontal scalability means that e.g. all rows belonging to a certain client are stored in one data center and rows belonging to another client in another data center.

Outsourcing of the data base layer without moving the whole application to the Cloud. In cases when the increased latency is tolerable, this is an easy strategy to move data silos to the Cloud and allow more clients to access the data. The local client then accesses the remote Cloud data store.
In order to run ressource demanding data analysis without effecting the production server, the data base layer can be cloned and the analysis can be done on the copy.
Copying of data from a Cloud service (access via API) to a local data store in order to work on the data with low latency.
In order to reduce latency the data is moved close to the client and/or to the data processing unit. Usually the whole application itself is migrated to the Cloud as well.

Google Cloud SQL is a web service that allows you to create, configure, and use relational databases that live in Google's cloud. It is a fully-managed service that maintains, manages, and administers your databases, allowing you to focus on your applications and services.

By offering the capabilities of a familiar MySQL database, the service enables you to easily move your data, applications, and services in and out of the cloud. This enables high data portability and helps you achieve faster time-to-market because you can quickly leverage your existing database.
up to 4 GB RAM, up to 10 GB storage

If the source and target system are compatible it can be sufficient to change the connection strings of all applications using the old source system to point to the address of the new target system. Make sure that not only applications that use the old source system are adapted, but also failover configurations, monitoring scripts and backup scripts.

If the type of the data store (RDBMS, NoSQL, Blob) changes during the data migration it is usually not sufficient enough to simulate missing functionality in the data access layer. If for example the schema is no fixed, or joins, strict consistency and ACID transactions are not supported any more, the application logic has to be adapted to reflect the conceptual changes. In case of migrating from a RDBMS to a NoSQL and Blob store, a small part of the data could still be required to be stored in a RDBMS to support e.g. financial transactions.
Some data stores have a different semantic what a database and schema is (e.g. Oracle and SQL Server). This can be translated by the data access layer without adapting any upper application layers.

If the product of the data store changes during the migration the semantics of e.g. comparison operators could have changed which cannot be translated by the data access layer and requires the application layer to be adopted.

If the target system is a NoSQL datastore and supports sorting of result sets, it may be necessary to add zero-padding for numbers and an offset to columns with negative numbers if the ordering is done in lexicographical order.

If the new target system is available under a different IP address or port then firewall settings need to be updated to allow all applications to access the new target system. It can also be useful to setup a Virtual Private Cloud (VPC).  Make sure that not only the firewall settings for applications that use the old source system are adapted, but also the firewall settings for failover configurations, monitoring scripts and backup scripts.

Despite SQL being a standardised language, most vendors add product specific SQL commands or differ in the semantics of e.g. locks to allow dirty reads or more fine grained locks. These differences have to be identified during the migration and applied to the application layer.

If the source and target system are compatible and the source system is currently addressed via an URL, a change of the CNAME entry to point to the new target system is sufficient to allow all applications to use the new target system.
If the source and target system are not compatible, Cloud Data Patterns can be used to simulate missing functionality, e.g. non-standard SQL commands.

In the geographic replication scenario the data access layer and above layers like the application layer have to be aware that the same layers run in parallel at different Cloud data centers and that the database layer has to be synchronized in the background.
In the data synchronisation scenario the synchronisation between the local replica and the Cloud master database has to be setup. This can be done by a synchronisation functionality of the RDBMS or by the data access layer or an external synchronisation tool.

If Cloud Data Patterns for Confidentiality are used in the data access layer, the application layer has to be aware that it might not have a full view on the actual data, since it could be anonymized, filtered or pseudonymized.

Most data stores have different naming conventions, therefore short, uppercase identifiers (table names, row names, constraint names) without special characters and potentially reserved words should be used. The data access layer can transform denied identifiers on the fly to allowed ones without the need to adapt upper application layers.

NoSQL data stores usually don't support complex queries using joins and aggregate functions on the data store level, but there are external services that could be used to defere complex queries on NoSQL data stores, e.g. using a MapReduce cluster. Using those services located closely to the data store can increase performance and reduce traffic compared to simulating complex queries on the client.

Some databases don't support null values for data entries, if the previous data store supported these, the data access layer can in some cases resolve that conflict if the applications itself don't differentiate between 'null' and empty values. If the applications differentiate between 'null' and empty values, the upper application layers have to be adopted.
Most data stores support different sets of data types which the data access layer can transform on the fly without the need to change upper application layers (e.g. BOOLEAN to BIT or CHAR(1)).
If the CNAME entry can be adapted to point to the new target system, make sure the DNS cache of all applications that use the old source system have a short time to live (TTL).

In the Cloud Burst scenario the data access layer can trigger the Cloud Burst case as well as the remigration back to the local data center. This decision can also be made by an external monitoring tool that calls the (re)migration scripts.

If the target system is a NoSQL datastore and allows only small values for attributes (e.g. max. 1kB), then larger values have to be stored in a Blob store and a pointer to the location in the Blob store can work as an intermediary attribute.

Does the source system support transactions and are these used by the application?
Does the source system support Joins and are there used by the application?
What is the data store type of the source system?
Is the source system addressed on the network level via an URL that can be resolved by a DNS?
Does the source system support stored procedures or trigger and are these used by the application?
Does the source system have a synchronisation function?
Does the source system run on a distributed infrastructure?

The Cloud Data Migration Tool (CDM) allows for migrating the data layer of an application to a cloud data store. The application itself is a Java Web application which uses a local MySQL database as data layer. The migration of the whole application to the Cloud should allow to use a compatible data store in the cloud.

If your migration scenario requires scalability of your target system, but it isn't, you can adapt your application logic to switch between read and write replicas.
If your migration scenario requires scalability of your target system, but it isn't, you can adapt your application logic to switch between read and write replicas.
If your target system does not ensure confidentiality, you can adapt your data access layer to prohibit storing sensitive data there.
If your target system is hosted in a public cloud, you can use a cloud data pattern called 'Pseudonymizer of Critical Data' to ensure your critical data is not exposed.

If your current system is a RDBMS and uses stored procedures or triggers and your target system is a NoSQL data store that does not support stored procedures or triggers, you can adapt your application logic to process the stored procedure and trigger logic.
If your target system is hosted in a public cloud, you can use a cloud data pattern called 'Anonymizer of Critical Data' to ensure your critical data is not exposed.
If your target system is hosted in a public cloud, you can adapt your application logic to prohibit storing sensitive data there.
If your migration scenario requires sharding functionality in your target system, but it isn't supported, you can use a cloud data pattern called "Local Sharding-Based Router".
If your target system does not ensure confidentiality, you can use a cloud data pattern called 'Pseudonymizer of Critical Data'.

If your source system is a RDBMS and target system a NoSQL data store and you use complex SQL queries which are not supported in the NoSQL store, you can adapt your application logic to use simpler request and applying the query logic on the client side.
If your migration scenario requires sharding functionality in your target system, but it isn't supported, you can adapt your data access layer to select the right shard for every query.
If your migration scenario requires sharding functionality in your target system, but it isn't supported, you can adapt your application logic to select the right shard for every query.

If your current system is different (product) than your target system, then a cloud data pattern called "Data Store Functionality Extension" can simulate features and semantics that the target system does not understand or would handle differently.
If your target system is hosted in a public cloud, you can use a cloud data pattern called 'Filter of Critical Data' to ensure your critical data is not exposed.
If your migration scenario requires scalability of your target system, but it isn't, you can adapt your data access layer to switch between read and write replicas.
If your target system is hosted in a public cloud, you can use a cloud data pattern called 'Confidentiality Level Data Aggregator' to ensure your critical data is not exposed.
If your target system does not ensure confidentiality, you can use a cloud data pattern called 'Anonymizer of Critical Data'.
If your target system is hosted in a public cloud, you can adapt your data access layer to prohibit storing sensitive data there.

If your source and target system is addressed via a DNS resolvable URL and the source and target data store are compatible you can adapt the connection strings in the application settings and configure your firewall to allow access to the target data store.
If your target system does not ensure confidentiality, you can adapt your application layer to prohibit storing sensitive data there.
If your migration scenario requires scalability of your target system, but it isn't, you can use a cloud data pattern called "Local Database Proyx".
If your migration scenario requires sharding functionality in your target system, but it isn't supported, you can use a cloud data pattern called "Local Sharding-Based Router".

If your current system is different (version) than your target system, then you can adapt your application logic to not use features that are not supported and use different semantics as required by the target system.
If your target system does not ensure confidentiality, you can use a cloud data pattern called 'Confidentiality Level Data Aggregator'.
If your migration scenario requires scalability of your target system, but it isn't, you can use a cloud data pattern called "Local Database Proyx".
If your target system does not support high availability and high scalability of read requests then you can instanciate multiple instances and setup synchronization between them.
If your migration scenario requires one-way syncing but the source or target system does not support it, you can adapt your application logic to sync the data manually.

If your source and target system is addressed via a DNS resolvable URL and the source and target data store are compatible you can just change the CNAME entry to point to the new target system, check that the DNS Cache TTL is low and configure your firewall to allow access to the target data store.

If your current system is a RDBMS and uses stored procedures or triggers and your target system is a NoSQL data store that does not support stored procedures or triggers, you can adapt your data access layer to process the stored procedure and trigger logic.
If your current system is different (version) than your target system, then you can adapt your data access layer to simulate the missing features and translate the different semantics.

If your source and target system is addressed via IP and the source and target data store are compatible you can adapt the connection strings in the application settings and configure your firewall to allow access to the target data store.
If your migration scenario requires sharding functionality in your target system, but it isn't supported, you can adapt your application logic to select the right shard for every query.
If your target system does not ensure confidentiality, you can use a cloud data pattern called 'Filter of Critical Data'.
If your migration scenario requires scalability of your target system, but it isn't, you can adapt your data access layer to switch between read and write replicas.
If your migration scenario requires two-way syncing but the source or target system does not support it, you can adapt your data access layer to sync the data manually.

If your current system is different (version) than your target system, then a cloud data pattern called "Data Store Functionality Extension" can simulate features and semantics that the target system does not understand or would handle differently.
If your current system is different (product) than your target system, then you can adapt your data access layer to simulate the missing features and translate the different semantics.
If your migration scenario requires two-way syncing but the source or target system does not support it, you can use ETL or third party migration tools to im- and export the data manually.
If your migration scenario requires one-way syncing but the source or target system does not support it, you can use ETL or third party migration tools to im- and export the data manually.

If your current system is a RDBMS and uses stored procedures or triggers and your target system is a NoSQL data store that does not support stored procedures or triggers, you can use a cloud data pattern called "Emulator of Stored Procedures" that interprets stored procedures and triggers.

If your source system is a RDBMS and target system a NoSQL data store and you use complex SQL queries which are not supported in the NoSQL store, you can use external services that allow complex queries on NoSQL databases like AWS Elastic MapReduce.
If your migration scenario requires one-way syncing but the source or target system does not support it, you can adapt your data access layer to sync the data manually.
If your resource utilization can grow faster than it takes to launch a new instance, you can use a pool of spare resource to have constant response time.

If your source system is a RDBMS and target system a NoSQL data store and you use complex SQL queries which are not supported in the NoSQL store, you can adapt your data access layer to simulate those complex queries by splitting them into simpler request and applying the query logic on the client side.
If your migration scenario requires sharding functionality in your target system, but it isn't supported, you can adapt your data access layer to select the right shard for every query.
If your migration scenario requires two-way syncing but the source or target system does not support it, you can adapt your application logic to sync the data manually.

If your current system is different (product) than your target system, then you can adapt your application logic to not use features that are not supported and use different semantics as required by the target system.
